summaries.content: This lesson introduces division as equal sharing. Students learn that when we share things equally, everyone gets the same amount. For example, if 12 rotis are shared among 4 people, each person gets 3 rotis. Students practise sharing objects like sweets, pencils, and fruits among friends and learn to write this as a division sentence using the ÷ symbol.
keyPoints: Division means splitting a total into equal groups
Equal sharing gives the same amount to each person
The ÷ symbol is used to write division
12 ÷ 4 = 3 means 12 shared among 4 gives 3 each
You can share by giving one item at a time to each person

summaries.content: This lesson teaches division facts and how they connect to multiplication tables. If students know that 5 x 7 = 35, they can figure out that 35 ÷ 5 = 7 and 35 ÷ 7 = 5. Students learn the names of the parts of a division problem: dividend, divisor, and quotient. They also learn about remainders — what is left over when things cannot be shared perfectly equally.
keyPoints: Every multiplication fact gives two division facts
The dividend is the total, the divisor is the number of groups, the quotient is the answer
A remainder is what is left over after equal sharing
The remainder is always smaller than the divisor
You can use multiplication tables to find division answers quickly

summaries.content: This lesson explores division through measurement and real-life sharing. Students solve problems like cutting a ribbon into equal pieces, packing items into bags, and sharing milk among families. They learn the difference between 'sharing' (dividing into a given number of groups) and 'grouping' (making groups of a given size). Students also learn to check their division answer using multiplication.
keyPoints: Division is used in measuring — cutting lengths, sharing weights, and dividing quantities
Sharing: You know how many groups, you find the size of each group
Grouping: You know the size of each group, you find how many groups
To check division, multiply the quotient by the divisor to get the dividend
If there is a remainder: (quotient x divisor) + remainder = dividend

summaries.content: This lesson brings together all the ideas about sharing and measuring with division. Students practise special division rules: dividing by 1 gives the same number, dividing a number by itself gives 1, and 0 divided by any number is 0. They solve real-world word problems involving fair sharing in school, at home, and in the market, building confidence with division in everyday life.
keyPoints: Any number ÷ 1 = the number itself
Any number ÷ itself = 1
0 ÷ any number = 0
Division helps solve real-world problems about fair sharing
Always check your division answer using multiplication
